justification: Intergenerational mobility perception is inherently multi-dimensional, encompassing both retrospective assessments (past mobility) and prospective expectations (future mobility), so no single item captures the full construct. The questions span 6–8 category scales with moderate-to-good entropy, making them broadly compatible for a latent-variable reduction approach. PCA on the first component extracts the dominant shared variance across all five items while handling minor scale-range differences, and binning to 5 quantiles yields a well-distributed ordinal target suitable for the bridge model.

Poverty attribution and definition is a conceptually rich construct covering both causal beliefs (structural vs. individual responsibility) and definitional thresholds, requiring multiple items to represent it adequately. p27_1 is excluded because its 10-category scale with the highest entropy (2.82 bits) likely reflects a distinct distributional format that could distort a simple composite, while p25, p26, p39, and p40 share comparable 6–9 category ordinal structures. PCA on the first component captures the dominant attitudinal dimension across these four items and bins cleanly to 5 quantiles for the bridge regression.

Social policy preferences require aggregation because attitudes toward redistribution, targeting, and program design are distinct but correlated facets of a single latent preference dimension. p29_1, p41, p42, and p43 share broadly comparable ordinal scales (6–8 categories) and collectively cover the breadth of the construct; p44 is excluded because its 4-category scale with low entropy (1.20 bits) adds little information and would compress the composite range. Mean-scoring these four items and binning to ≤5 categories produces a stable, interpretable ordinal variable with good spread for the bridge model.

With only three items available, all three should be retained to maximize construct coverage across the inequality-perception and discrimination-experience dimensions. The scales are narrow (4–5 categories) and the entropy of p37 is notably low (0.55 bits), suggesting a skewed but substantively important item that complements the more evenly distributed p32 and p33. Summing the three items and binning to ≤5 categories is appropriate given the small number of items and limited scale range, producing an ordinal composite that captures cumulative perceived disadvantage.

Subjective economic wellbeing and agency is a multi-faceted construct spanning current financial situation, perceived control, and economic trajectory, so aggregation across all five items is warranted. p13–p16 share an identical 13-category scale with high entropy, while p10 uses a 7-category scale; PCA accommodates this mild scale heterogeneity by extracting the dominant latent dimension rather than assuming strict additivity. Binning the first principal component to 5 quantiles yields a well-spread ordinal variable that efficiently summarizes the shared variance in subjective economic experience for the bridge regression.
